s: There are three people in my family: Dad, Mom, and me.
At noon we eat at home.
Mom made a lot of dishes and rice.
I ate a lot of rice and drank a cup of water.
In the afternoon I go to the shop with Mom.
There are a lot of things in the shop.
We buy six apples and some water.
The apples are ten kuai, and the water is three kuai.
Mom says: "These apples are very good."
I really like going to the shop with Mom.
Mom says: "Let's go to the hospital."
The doctor takes a look at me.
The doctor says: "Please drink more water and sleep more."
Tomorrow I want to go to school.
I am American, and I work in China.
I can speak a little Chinese.
Today at noon I go to a restaurant to eat.
I say, "I'll have rice and tea."
The man at the restaurant hears this and is very pleased; he says, "Your Chinese is very good!"
I am very happy — I love studying Chinese.
My family has a little cat called Mimi.
Mimi is very small and loves to sleep.
At noon when I eat, it sits on my chair.
In the afternoon it likes to watch the little dogs outside.
Mimi is my good friend — I love it very much.
I get up at seven o'clock.
At noon I eat with my friends.
In the afternoon I study at home.
In the evening I read, and at eleven I go to bed.
My family has a little dog called Doudou.
Doudou is two years old and loves to eat.
Every morning it goes running with me.
When I come home, it gets very happy.
I love Doudou — it is my good friend.
There are four people in my family.
My dad is a doctor, and my mom is a teacher.
I have a younger sister; she is eight years old this year.
We all love the food my mom cooks.
I have a good friend called Li Yue.
Li Yue is a doctor; she works at a hospital.
She loves reading, and she also loves cooking.
The food she makes is delicious, and we are both very happy.
My room isn't big, but it's very pretty.
In the room there is a desk and a chair.
On the desk there is a computer and some books.
I like to sit on the chair, reading and writing characters.
My cat likes to sleep in my room.
I am a student, and my school is big.
There are many classmates and teachers in the school.
In the morning we study Chinese, and in the afternoon we read and write characters.
My teacher is very nice, and the classmates are all happy.
In front of the school there is a small shop, and I like to go there to buy water.
I love my school very much.
Teacher Li is my Chinese teacher.
She is in her thirties and very pretty.
She speaks Chinese, and also a little English.
When we can't write characters, she teaches us how.
Yesterday afternoon, I missed my mom very much.
I made a phone call and said, "Hello, Mom, how are you?"
Mom said, "I'm fine! Have you eaten yet?"
I said, "I have. I'm studying Chinese at school, and I'm very happy."
Mom said, "Good. Come home for a meal on Sunday, okay?"
At noon we go to a restaurant to eat.
I order a bowl of rice and some dishes.
My friend drinks water; I drink tea.
The food is delicious, and we are all very happy.
The restaurant staff says: "Welcome back again!"
Hello! My name is Wang Xiaoming.
I am a student, twenty years old this year.
My family is my dad, my mom and me.
I like reading books and watching movies.
In the afternoon I go to the store to buy something.
I want to buy a cup.
This cup is very pretty — how much is it?
I really like my new cup.
This morning I am going to the train station.
I ask the driver: "How much is it?"
Twenty minutes later, I see the train station.
In the morning I like to drink coffee; in the afternoon I like to drink tea.
Today a friend comes to my home.
I say, "Would you like tea or coffee?"
He says, "I don't drink coffee — I love hot tea."
We drink tea together, and we are both very happy.
Today is Saturday, and I don't have to work.
In the afternoon, my friend and I went to see a movie.
The movie was really good, and we both loved it.
While watching the movie, we drank tea and ate apples.
The people in the movie spoke Chinese, and I could catch a little of it!
Tomorrow I want to watch another movie.
The weather is great today — not cold and not hot.
It rained yesterday, but today it has stopped.
My mom and I go out for a walk.
There are lots of people outside, all having fun.
In the morning I am sleeping, and Mom calls me.
I say, "What time is it now?"
Mom says, "It's eight o'clock! You go to work at nine!"
I don't eat breakfast — I just drink a little water.
At 8:40 I take a taxi to work.
Tomorrow I will get up at seven!
At school I got to know a classmate.
I say: "My name is Wang Xiaoming. And you?"
He says: "My name is Li Ming."
He is twenty years old and is Chinese.
Yesterday it rained and the weather was cold, so I stayed home and read.
Today it isn't raining, and the weather is lovely.
In the morning I went to school to study, and at noon I ate rice with my classmates.
Tomorrow is Sunday, so I won't go to school.
I want to go to the store to buy some clothes.
Yesterday, today, and tomorrow — I'm happy every day.
This afternoon I went to visit my friend's new home.
But after walking for ten minutes, I couldn't find the way anymore.
I asked a man nearby, "Excuse me, how do I get to Beijing Road?"
He said, "Go straight ahead, turn left at the traffic light, and you're there."
I asked him, "Is it far from here?"
He said with a smile, "Not far — a five-minute walk and you're there."
Soon I arrived at my friend's home, and he was very happy to see me.
At eight in the evening, we arrived at our hotel in Beijing.
The attendant asked: "Hello, sir, what is your name?"
I told him my name, and he gave us a big room.
The room is big and new; we both like it a lot.
My wife said she was tired and wanted to rest.
I drank a cup of tea, glanced at my phone, and then went to sleep.
In the morning I go to the coffee shop to buy coffee.
The server asks me: "Would you like it hot or iced?"
I say: "I'd like a hot coffee, no sugar."
The coffee is thirty kuai — a little expensive.
But it's delicious — I want to drink it every day.
This morning I went to a store to buy clothes.
I saw a white top — very pretty, but it cost three hundred kuai.
I asked the shop assistant: "Do you have a slightly cheaper one?"
She gave me a black top — one hundred fifty kuai, cheaper than the white one.
The black top was also very pretty, so I bought it.
I think cheap things can be pretty too.
Last night my friend and I went out for hotpot — it was my first time eating it.
The restaurant was crowded and very hot.
On the table there were lamb, fish, and noodles.
My friend told me, "The meat is ready quickly in the pot — don't wait too long."
I ate a piece of lamb — it was so delicious, but also really spicy!
I drank a lot of water, and my eyes even turned red.
I said to my friend, "Next time I want to come again!"
My older brother lives in Shanghai, and today he is coming to Beijing.
I got up at six in the morning and took a taxi to the airport.
There were a lot of cars on the road, so we drove very slowly.
I got to the airport at nine, but my brother's plane had not arrived yet.
I bought a cup of coffee and sat there waiting for him.
At ten thirty my brother came out; he smiled and said, "Little brother, I missed you so much!"
Every Saturday I go play basketball.
We play from nine to eleven.
After the game, everyone drinks cola and eats watermelon together.
Last year my health wasn't very good, and I didn't like exercising either.
The doctor told me, "Running is good for your health — you should exercise more."
Now I get up at six every morning and go running in the park next to my home.
At the beginning, I would get tired after running for just ten minutes.
Now I can run for thirty minutes, and I also run faster than before.
The park is beautiful in the morning — birds are singing, and there are many people out running.
Exercise is making my health better day by day.
At noon my classmate and I go to a restaurant to eat.
The server asks us: "What would you like to eat?"
I ordered fish, and my classmate ordered lamb and rice.
We also ordered two cups of tea.
The food came quickly, and the fish was really delicious.
After finishing the meal, we went right back to school for class.
This restaurant's food is tasty and cheap; we want to come again next time.
My husband goes to play football every Sunday.
This Sunday, he asked me to come and watch too.
The weather was great; they played behind the school, and many people were watching.
My husband ran very fast and kicked in two goals.
Everyone laughed and said, "That was great!"
On the way home he was very tired, but extremely happy.
He asked me, "Will you come again next week?" I said, "Yes, I will!"
Last night I felt really unwell — maybe I was getting sick.
This morning I didn't go to work; I went to the hospital.
I said, "I have a headache, and I've got a fever too."
The doctor gave me some medicine and told me to drink more water and get more rest.
I slept for a whole day, and now I feel much better.
On Saturday evening, my friends and I went out singing together.
My friend Xiao Wang sings extremely well; everyone loves listening to him.
Although I don't sing well, I sing very happily.
When the singing was done, everyone began to dance.
Xiao Wang dances better than I do, so I watched him and slowly learned.
By the time we went home it was already twelve o'clock, but everyone was very happy.
My company is far from home, so I take the bus to work every day.
It was raining this morning, and a lot of people were waiting for the bus at the stop.
The bus came, and everyone got on.
There were no seats on the bus, so I stood for twenty minutes.
A student said to me, "I'm getting off soon — take my seat."
I said, "Thank you!" The rain was heavy, but I felt happy.
Next month my wife and I want to travel to Beijing.
This is our travel plan: we intend to spend a week having fun.
I bought the plane tickets on my phone; they were not too expensive.
We also found a hotel that is very close to the train station.
My wife says Beijing has lots of tasty food, and she wants to try it all.
I hope the weather is sunny so we can have a very happy time.
On Saturday afternoon I go to my friend's home as a guest.
I bought some apples and a watermelon to give him.
My friend's mom cooked a lot of delicious dishes.
We ate together and talked a lot.
The kitten at his home is very pretty; I like it a lot.
At nine in the evening I went home; today I had a really great time.
Every evening, I watch the weather forecast.
Yesterday the TV said, "Tomorrow there will be rain, and it will be very cold."
This morning when I left home, the sky was overcast, so I put on a lot of clothes.
But at noon it didn't rain — the sky turned sunny, and it was even hot!
I was wearing too much; after walking just a little I was so hot I didn't even want to talk.
I smiled and thought: sometimes the weather forecast can be wrong too.
Tomorrow I will still watch the weather forecast, but I will also take a look at the sky myself!
On Saturday the weather is great.
My girlfriend and I go to the park together.
There are lots of people in the park — some are jogging, some are singing.
We sit under a tree, drinking tea and chatting.
Time passes so quickly — we spend the whole afternoon having fun.
Tomorrow is my friend's birthday, and in the evening we are all going to have dinner together.
Tonight I asked my husband, "What should I wear tomorrow?"
He said, "Wear that red one — you look the most beautiful in red."
I said, "I bought that one last year — isn't it too big now?"
My husband smiled and said, "It's not too big — you look good in anything."
I was very happy and said, "Okay, I'll wear it tomorrow!"
He said, "Stop thinking about it and go to sleep — tomorrow you will be the most beautiful one there!"
I've lived in Beijing for two years now, and I've found the four seasons here are all very different.
In spring, the flowers in the parks all bloom, but the wind is a bit strong.
Summer is especially hot; in the evenings people like to sit outside eating and chatting.
Autumn is Beijing's best season — the sky is blue, and it's neither cold nor hot.
In autumn, many people go to the parks to see the leaves that have turned red.
Winter is very cold; it's often windy, and sometimes it even snows.
Last winter I saw snow for the first time and was as happy as a child.
If you want to visit Beijing, let me tell you: autumn is the best time to come!
Next Monday, I'm going to Shanghai on a business trip with my manager.
It's my first business trip, so I feel both excited and a little worried.
On Sunday evening, I put my clothes and my computer into the suitcase.
At six o'clock the next morning, we took a taxi to the airport.
On the plane, the manager told me: "When we arrive in Shanghai, we'll go to the hotel first, then go meet the clients."
In the afternoon we had a three-hour meeting, and the clients were very satisfied with us.
In the evening, the manager treated me to Shanghai's most famous dishes.
The business trip ended quickly; I feel I learned a lot — it was really great!
Last year I made a Chinese friend named Xiaolong.
He is especially kind to me and often helps me practice Chinese.
When we eat together, he teaches me how to use chopsticks.
At first I was terrible at it, and everyone laughed.
Now I can not only use chopsticks, but also cook a few Chinese dishes.
It's so good to have friends!
The weather was great on Saturday, so three coworkers and I went mountain climbing.
At eight in the morning we met at the foot of the mountain, and everyone brought water and bread.
At first I climbed fast, but later my legs got more and more tired.
A coworker said to me, "Don't rush — let's climb slowly."
At twelve noon, we finally reached the top of the mountain.
Looking down from the mountain, the buildings in the city looked tiny — it was incredibly beautiful.
We ate our bread at the top and rested for half an hour.
Although my legs ached after I got home, I still want to climb again next month.
I used to get up at seven thirty every morning, and I was always in a hurry.
I often looked for clothes while brushing my teeth, and sometimes I went out the door having forgotten to wash my face.
Starting last month, I made myself get up at six fifty.
Now I first brush my teeth and wash my face, then quietly eat breakfast and drink a cup of hot milk.
After finishing my meal, I even have time to watch the news for a while.
Now this has become my habit, and I'm never in a hurry in the morning any more.
Who knew that getting up forty minutes earlier would make my whole day different.
Today was my first day at work, and I got up as early as six thirty.
When I arrived at the company, there wasn't a single person in the office.
At nine o'clock the manager introduced me to everyone, and my colleagues were all very warm.
A colleague surnamed Wang taught me how to use the company's computer.
At noon, she even treated me to noodles downstairs.
In the afternoon I attended my first meeting; there was a lot I couldn't understand, but I wrote it all down in my notebook.
On my way home in the evening, I was both tired and happy.
Last weekend, I rode my bike to the park alone to have some fun.
On the way home, it got dark, and I found that I was lost.
My phone was out of battery too, so I couldn't check the map, and I was extremely anxious.
I asked an elderly man by the road: "Excuse me, how do I get to Zhongshan Road?"
He said warmly: "Keep walking straight ahead; when you reach the traffic light, walk to the right for ten minutes and you'll be there."
I soon found the way home, and my heart was finally at ease.
From now on, every time I go out, I will check the map carefully first.
Last night I had a slight fever, and my head hurt a lot too.
This morning, I went to the hospital with a colleague to see a doctor.
The hospital was really crowded; we first registered on the first floor.
While registering, the lady asked me: "Where do you feel unwell?"
I said: "I have a fever; it's probably a cold."
The doctor examined me and said it was nothing serious, telling me to drink more water and get more rest.
After picking up the medicine, I went home to sleep, and now I feel much better.
Last Wednesday I went to a company for a job interview.
The evening before, I prepared a lot of questions and didn't go to sleep until very late.
In the morning I got up very early, because I didn't want to be late.
I wore a white shirt, hoping the manager would think I was serious.
The manager asked me: "Why do you want to come work at our company?"
I was a little nervous, but I answered every question carefully.
When the interview ended, the manager said with a smile: "Not bad. Go home and wait for our call."
Yesterday they really did call — next month I'll be starting work!
I love eating Chinese food and have always wanted to learn how to cook it.
Last weekend, my Chinese friend invited me to her home and taught me to make tomato and egg stir-fry.
First we went to the supermarket and bought eggs and tomatoes.
My friend said, "This dish is easy, but don't forget to add the salt."
The first dish I made was too salty; my friend took one bite and laughed.
The second time I used a little less salt, and it turned out much better than the first.
That evening we ate and chatted at the same time — the time flew by.
Now I can make this dish, and next week I want to cook it for my mom.
One day in March, I discovered that last year's pants no longer fit.
My wife said with a smile: "You really have gotten fat."
I decided to start losing weight from that day on.
Now I go running in the park at six every morning, and I no longer eat cake or sweet things.
At first it was very hard — I was often both hungry and tired.
But after three months, I had lost five kilograms, and my health kept getting better and better.
Yesterday I put those pants on again, and my wife said I look much younger than before!
Last week we moved, because the old place was too far from my office.
Although the new home isn't big, it's both clean and quiet.
On moving day, a few friends came to help us carry boxes, and everyone was worn out.
At noon we didn't have a table yet, so we just sat on the floor and ate noodles.
In the evening, the neighbors brought over some fruit and said, "Welcome!"
The new home is very close to the subway station — a five-minute walk and you're there.
Now I like this new home more and more.
Half a year ago, I moved to a new place.
Back then I didn't know anyone; every day after work I came home to a very quiet house.
My neighbor is an auntie in her sixties who exercises by the entrance every morning.
One day she brought me a bowl of hot noodles and said: "Even living alone, you should still eat properly."
On weekends, I help her carry heavy things upstairs.
Now we often drink tea and chat together.
When my mom asked whether I was used to living alone, I said: "Don't worry — I have a neighbor who is just like family."
I just came to Beijing for work, and this morning I went to the bank to open an account.
There were a lot of people in the bank; after taking a number, I sat waiting for half an hour.
The staff member asked me: "Did you bring your passport?"
I said: "Yes, here are my passport and my phone number."
She had me write my name and also asked me to think of a password.
Fifteen minutes later, I got my new bank card.
With a bank card, I can now spend money with my phone to buy things — so convenient!
I used to take the bus to work; the roads were full of cars and I was often late.
A coworker asked me, "Why don't you take the subway?"
I tried it once and found the subway is both fast and cheap.
From home to the office takes only twenty minutes — much faster than the bus.
On the subway I can read a book or listen to music.
The subway station is very clean, and everyone lines up to board.
Last week it rained hard and the cars on the road moved especially slowly, but the subway was still fast.
Now I take the subway to work every day and haven't been late once — the subway is so convenient!
Last month I found a new job, so this Saturday I treated my friends to dinner.
We went to a restaurant near my company; the food there is both tasty and cheap.
I looked at the menu first and ordered six dishes and some beer for everyone.
My friend Xiao Li doesn't drink beer; he only asked for a glass of sweet fruit juice.
We chatted while eating, and everyone was very happy.
After the meal, my friends wanted to pay, but I said with a smile: "Today it's my treat — I won't take anyone's money!"
They said next time they would treat me, and that's what we decided.
After graduating from university, everyone runs into an important question: what kind of career to choose.
My parents hope I will become a teacher, because the job is stable and the pressure is low.
But I am especially interested in computer technology and want to go to a big city to find a job related to the internet.
A friend told me that when choosing a career, salary matters of course, but interest matters even more.
If you spend every day doing things you don't like, no salary, however high, can make you happy.
I thought for a long time and decided to first try working at an internet company to gain some experience.
Although Mom and Dad were a little disappointed, in the end they still supported my decision.
A career is just like a pair of shoes: only your own feet know whether it fits.
A dozen or so years ago, few people in China drank coffee; everyone preferred tea.
But now, as life changes, more and more coffee shops are opening in every city.
For many young people, coffee is not just a drink.
The first cup in the morning is how work begins; the afternoon cup is a small gift to yourself.
Interestingly, coffee in China has developed its own flavor — coffee with tea, coffee with fruit.
Some say drinking coffee is copying Westerners; I feel, however, that Chinese people have turned coffee into a culture of their own.
Actually, neither tea nor coffee is better than the other — what matters is the moment you sit down and drink slowly.
Many young people are busy all day; only the evening hours belong to themselves.
So they don't want to sleep; they scroll on their phones while telling themselves, "Just ten more minutes."
As a result, ten minutes turns into two hours, and by the time they go to bed it is already the middle of the night.
Staying up late seems to give us a little freedom, but really it is borrowing time from our own body.
What you borrow must always be paid back: the next day your head hurts, you have no energy at work, and you can't do anything well.
I used to stay up late all the time too; not until I got sick and was hospitalized did I understand how important health is.
Now I go to bed at eleven every night, and when I get up in the morning the whole world feels brand new.
Go to bed a little earlier tonight — tomorrow's you will thank today's you.
Nowadays, ordering food delivery has already become part of many people's lives.
As long as you pick your dishes on your phone, half an hour later hot food arrives at your door.
Delivery is fast and convenient; it lets us skip cooking and skip going out.
But behind the convenience there is also a price.
Some people discover they haven't set foot in the kitchen for a whole year.
To be a little faster, delivery riders often ride dangerously.
I think it's fine to order delivery, but on weekends it's best to cook a meal yourself.
Because cooking is not only about eating — it is also the flavor of life itself.
Twenty years ago, the train from Beijing to Shanghai took more than ten hours.
Now the high-speed train takes only four and a half hours.
The high-speed train is fast and safe, the seats are comfortable, and the carriages are quiet.
You can drink tea and read on board, watching cities and mountains fly past the window.
What high-speed rail has changed is not only speed, but also people's lives.
Many people live in one city and work in another; going home by high-speed train on weekends to see their parents has also become easy.
Some people say the world has become smaller.
Actually the world hasn't changed — it is the distance between us and faraway places that has grown shorter.
When looking for a job, the interview is often more important than the resume.
Many people are quite capable, yet because they get too nervous, they can't get a single sentence out well in the interview.
Actually, there are techniques to interviewing.
First, before you go, learn about the company and find out what kind of person they need.
Second, when answering questions, don't just say "I work very hard" — it's best to give a real example.
In addition, dress neatly and arrive ten minutes early — these small things all leave a good impression.
Finally, don't forget: an interview is not only the company choosing you, but also you choosing the company.
Relax a little, treat the interview as a chat, and you will do even better.
It has already been ten years since I left my hometown.
When I first came to this big city, I assumed I would go back very soon.
Later, a job, friends, someone I love — one by one, they kept me here.
Every Spring Festival I take a twenty-hour train home, and my mother is always waiting for me at the door.
Her cooking still tastes the same, but her hair has turned so much whiter.
The city gave me a new life, yet my hometown keeps my oldest memories.
When someone asks me, "Where is your home?" — I cannot answer clearly.
Perhaps that is what a migrant is: someone who lives in a new place, but has left their heart somewhere far away.
Nowadays, the phone has become one of the most important things in our lives.
We use our phones to chat, shop, read the news — even to work.
But more and more people find they can't live without their phones.
They look at their phones while eating, and again before going to sleep.
I think we should put down our phones and spend more time with family and friends.
Long ago, there was a man who was extremely good at archery — he could hit the target nine times out of ten, and so he was very proud.
One day, an old oil seller stood nearby watching him shoot, and gave only a slight nod.
Annoyed, the man asked, "Do you really think my archery is not good?"
The old man said, "It is nothing special — you have simply practiced a lot."
With that, the old man set a bottle on the ground and placed on top of it a copper coin with a small hole in the middle.
Slowly he poured the oil through the small hole into the bottle, and not a drop of oil touched the coin.
The old man said with a smile, "I am nothing special either — I have simply poured oil many, many times."
The man understood: with anything at all, as long as you do it and practice it enough, you can do it both quickly and well.
This is what "practice makes perfect" means.
In today's China, a tiny QR code has changed almost every aspect of life.
Groceries, buses, restaurants, the doctor — as long as you take out your phone and scan, the payment is done in an instant.
In restaurants, the waiter no longer hands you a menu, but instead asks you to scan the QR code on the table to order.
The first time my grandma used a QR code to buy something, she was so nervous her hands were sweating.
Later she mastered it — nowadays even the old man selling fruit downstairs uses a QR code to collect money.
Of course, behind the convenience there are problems too: some elderly people have no smartphone, and shopping becomes very difficult for them.
So now many shops have a sign at the entrance: "Cash welcome."
New technology should make everyone's life more convenient, rather than convenient for only some people.
In China's big cities, queuing is one of the most common things in life: you queue to shop, you queue for the bus, and you queue at the hospital too.
Some people are very patient in line, looking at their phones while slowly waiting.
But some people don't want to wait: seeing many people ahead, they simply cut in line.
People who cut in line may think they are clever, but actually everyone silently resents them.
I once saw an elderly man politely say to a young man who had cut in line: "We are all waiting — would you please go to the back?"
The young man blushed, said "sorry," and immediately went to stand at the end of the line.
Queuing seems like a small matter, but it actually best shows a place's level of civility.
If you are willing to wait three minutes, others will be willing to wait three minutes for you too.
Last month I changed jobs, and the new company was too far from my old home, so I decided to rent a place near the office.
There was plenty of information online, but some photos were completely different from the real thing.
I looked at five apartments; they were either too expensive or too old.
Finally I found a suitable room, only one stop from the office, with a clean kitchen and bathroom.
The landlord was a friendly auntie who said: "The rent is two thousand a month, and you pay for water and electricity yourself."
I discussed with her whether it could be a bit cheaper, and she smiled and agreed to charge one hundred kuai less per month.
When signing the contract, I carefully read through every single clause.
The evening I moved into my new home, I felt both tired and happy, as if life were starting all over again.
A few years ago, shared bikes suddenly appeared on the streets of Chinese cities.
As long as you scan with your phone, you can ride a bike away — extremely convenient.
The subway station is still two kilometers from your office? Ten minutes by bike and you're there.
Shared bikes are not only cheap but also good for the environment, so many people stopped driving.
But problems came too: some people parked bikes in the middle of the road, and others even rode them home to keep.
Later, more and more bikes lay broken, and some streets looked like a "sea" of bicycles.
Shared bikes taught us something: a good idea still needs everyone to take care of it together.
Nowadays people's lives move faster and faster, and the stress keeps growing too.
Work, housing, the children's education — every single thing makes people tense.
Many people assume that as long as they work hard, they will get to live an easy life.
But they discover that the busier they get, the greater the stress — yet their happiness keeps shrinking.
Actually, relaxing is not wasting time — it is refueling yourself.
You can go out for a little walk, listen to some music, or have a chat with friends.
For me, the best way to relax is to turn off my phone in the evening and quietly read a book.
Remember: only when you have rested well can you go further.
In China, gift giving is an art: what to give and how to give it both follow particular rules.
For example, when visiting a friend's home you can bring fruit, and the number had better be even, because even numbers represent good things.
Some things must not be given casually: clocks are out, and so are umbrellas, because their pronunciation makes people think of unlucky meanings.
My foreign friend was very surprised to hear this; he said that in his country, you can give anything.
Actually, a gift doesn't need to be expensive; what matters is your thoughtfulness.
Receiving gifts has its culture too: many Chinese people will first politely say "you shouldn't have," and only then accept.
In the past, many people would not open a gift right away; today's young people often open it while saying thank you.
What gift giving really gives is not just things, but even more the affection between people.
Before my first trip alone, I was very worried: what if I got lost? What if I got sick?
My friends also said it wasn't safe for a girl to go off on her own.
But I bought a train ticket anyway and went to a small city in the south.
When you travel alone, no one makes decisions for you — where to go, what to eat, where to stay, you have to figure it all out yourself.
Sometimes I got lost and sometimes I took the wrong bus, but every single time warm-hearted people helped me.
When you are on your own, you notice the scenery along the way more, and you talk to strangers more easily.
When the trip ended, I discovered that I had gotten to know not only a new place, but also a new me.
It turns out I am braver than I thought.
As medical science develops, people are living longer and longer, and there are more and more elderly people in society.
Many countries are entering an "aging society," and this has already become an important social issue.
The more elderly people there are, the more people are needed to care for them, so naturally the pressure on young people grows too.
At the same time, with more people retired and fewer people working, the country's economic development will also be affected.
However, an aging population is not entirely a bad thing.
Many elderly people are healthy and rich in experience, and after retiring they can continue to contribute to society.
Some experts suggest raising the retirement age, while others suggest encouraging young people to have more children.
No matter what methods we use, we should respect the elderly and let them live happy, joyful lives.
Recently our company started using artificial intelligence to write reports, and my colleagues' reactions have been very different.
Some worry their jobs will be replaced by machines, while others feel they can finally work less overtime.
To be honest, I have both feelings.
The AI really is faster than me: it takes me two hours to write a summary, and it can finish one in two minutes.
But it also makes mistakes often, and it gets things wrong with such confidence that you simply cannot notice unless you check carefully.
So my job has slowly changed: before, I did the writing; now, I judge whether the machine wrote it correctly.
A senior colleague said that in history, every time a new technology appeared people feared unemployment, yet in the end new professions always emerged.
Those words may well be true, but the road from the old job to the new one often takes many years to walk.
I don't know what I will be doing ten years from now; all I can do is keep learning while I use these tools.
The first time I heard the term "carbon neutrality" was on the TV news, and to be honest, I understood none of it at the time.
Only after looking it up did I understand: simply put, however much carbon dioxide you emit, you must reduce or absorb that same amount, so the total equals zero.
China's goal is to achieve carbon neutrality before 2060, which means energy, transportation, and industry must all undergo enormous changes.
It sounds like something that concerns only governments and big companies, with little to do with ordinary people.
But think about it carefully: our everyday choices are actually all connected to it — drive or take the subway, turn on the air conditioner or open the window.
The buses below my building have all been switched to electric ones — so quiet that at first I couldn't even get used to it.
Of course, some people doubt it: if I alone use one less plastic bag, does it really make a difference?
One person's power is indeed small, but what policy can change is precisely the shared habits of hundreds of millions of people.
Even if all you do is turn off the lights when you leave, it still counts as taking part in this great undertaking whose results will only be visible decades from now.
On the night of Double Eleven, I had put more than thirty items in my shopping cart, and just before paying I suddenly asked myself: do I really need these?
Advertisements always tell us: buy this and you will be more beautiful, more successful, happier.
In a bad mood? Buy something. In a great mood? Then all the more reason to buy something to celebrate.
This is exactly what consumerism is: it turns shopping from satisfying needs into proving yourself.
I have a friend whose salary is not low, yet he spends it all every month, even borrowing money to buy the newest phone.
He says: "Everyone else has one and I don't — how embarrassing would that be."
The problem is, this kind of happiness comes fast and goes fast: after three weeks the new phone becomes just an ordinary old phone.
That night I turned off my phone and went to sleep first; the next day I looked at the cart again, and in the end kept only one pair of sneakers I truly needed.
After buying less, I did not feel my life got worse at all — on the contrary, I felt a great deal lighter.
Many people, after arriving abroad, go through a period of not fitting in — this is the so-called "culture shock."
At first everything feels fresh: the streets, the food, the language — even the way people talk is different from back home.
But after a few weeks, the feeling of novelty slowly fades, and all kinds of difficulties appear.
For example, you may not understand the local people's jokes, nor know how to communicate with them.
Some people therefore feel lonely, and even regret leaving their own country.
Psychologists believe this is a normal stage that almost every overseas student has been through.
If you want to adapt faster, the best way is to take the initiative to make friends and join more local activities.
As time goes on, you will find that both cultures can bring you joy.
So when you meet culture shock, don't be afraid — it is actually a part of growing up.
The "generation gap" refers to the differences between two generations in their ways of thinking and ways of life.
The parents' generation lives frugally and likes to save money, yet young people are willing to spend money to enjoy life.
When it comes to finding a job, parents hope their children will find stable work, yet the children value interest and freedom more.
Dating and marriage are also a common source of conflict: many parents are anxious, yet young people feel there is no need to marry too early.
Some children feel their parents don't understand them, and gradually become unwilling to share their true feelings with them.
Actually, it is quite normal for a generation gap to arise, because the two generations grew up in different times.
To reduce the generation gap, the key lies in communication: parents should listen carefully to their children's ideas, and children should have a bit more patience.
As long as both sides respect each other, the two generations can surely become good friends.
Going home for Spring Festival, I almost failed to recognize my own hometown.
When I was little, in front of our house was a dirt road, and whenever it rained there was mud everywhere.
Now it has become a wide paved road, and at night the streetlights make it as bright as day.
In the past, the young people of the village all went to big cities to work, leaving only the elderly and children behind.
In recent years quite a few people have come back; some run online shops selling hometown apples, and business is incredibly good.
My cousin is one of them; he says, "Rather than working for someone else in a big city, it's better to come home and build a career of your own."
Of course, there are changes that make me sad too: the primary school I attended has closed, because there are fewer and fewer children.
The old tree at the village entrance is still there, but the old folks playing chess under it are a different group now.
My hometown has become more convenient and more beautiful, but some things, once lost, can never be found again.
If you want to understand young people in China today, there are two words you must know: neijuan (involution) and tangping (lying flat).
"Involution" refers to a kind of meaningless competition: everyone works harder and harder, yet what they get does not increase.
For example, in some companies one person volunteers to work overtime, so everyone else has no choice but to follow; in the end everyone is more exhausted, yet salaries haven't risen a cent.
"Lying flat" is the answer to involution: since hard work is useless, I will lower my desires — no buying a house, no getting married, just doing the most basic work.
My cousin is exactly like this. He says: "Rather than keep competing in a big city, I'd rather go back to my hometown and live a quiet life."
Their parents' generation criticizes this attitude as too negative, but young people feel it is not laziness — it is a kind of clear-headedness.
The interesting thing is, many people who say they are going to lie flat still show up to work on time the next day.
Perhaps lying flat is more like a mood — a way of protecting oneself under pressure.
Those who completely lie flat are a minority; most people are somewhere between involution and lying flat, searching for a position they can accept.
At six in the morning, before the sky is fully light, the Lanzhou noodle shop downstairs from my home is already open.
The owner is from Gansu; he came to this city twenty years ago and raised two children with his own two hands.
I often sit by the window and watch him pull the noodles: a lump of dough stretches long and thin in his hands, turning into fine noodles in just a few seconds.
The noodles go into the pot, the aroma of beef soup slowly drifts out, and the whole little shop fills with steam.
A proper bowl of Lanzhou noodles follows the rule of "one clear, two white, three red, four green": clear soup, white radish, red chili, green cilantro.
The owner says the soup starts simmering at three in the morning; the beef bones must be boiled for more than five hours.
Most customers are people rushing to work: they finish a bowl in ten minutes, heads covered in sweat, then walk out into the cold wind.
Someone once asked the owner why he doesn't open a few more shops and earn a bit more money.
He smiled and shook his head: "Open too many shops, and the soup won't taste like this anymore."
Behind every bowl of good noodles, there are decades of one person's patience.
When I moved house last year, I counted and found I somehow owned more than two hundred pieces of clothing — many still with the tags on.
In that moment, I decided to try the "minimalist living" my friend often talked about.
Minimalism is not buying nothing at all; it is keeping only the things you truly need and truly love.
I used a simple standard: anything I hadn't used in a year could basically be given away or thrown out.
The sorting process was painful; several times I stood holding an old sweater thinking: what if I need it someday?
The facts proved that this "someday" almost never came.
Now my room is half empty: finding things is faster, cleaning is easier, and even the time spent choosing clothes in the morning has gotten shorter.
Even more unexpectedly, once I owned less, I became more careful about buying: before every purchase, I ask myself three times.
For me, minimalism is not the goal itself, but a method for letting life return to what matters.
With the development of the internet, online shopping has become more and more common.
Without leaving home, people can buy products from all over the world.
Although online shopping is convenient, it also has some problems.
For example, some products are of poor quality and look completely different from the pictures.
Therefore, consumers should stay calm when shopping and avoid spending blindly.
After the epidemic, our company allowed employees to work from home three days a week.
At first, I thought it was the happiest thing in the world: no squeezing onto the subway, no formal clothes, and I could even pet the cat during meetings.
But three months later, I discovered things were not that simple.
At home, the boundary between work and life became more and more blurry.
At ten in the evening, colleagues are still sending messages in the group chat — do you reply or not?
I also began to miss the boring little things at the office: lunch with colleagues at noon, someone treating everyone to coffee in the afternoon.
It turns out what I missed was not the office, but the warmth between people.
Now I go to the company two days a week and work from home three days, and I have slowly found a balance.
Remote work is not that good, nor that bad — it is simply a new way of living that we have to learn to manage ourselves.
Before going to sleep at night, I tell myself: only ten minutes of short videos.
By the time I look at the clock again, it is already one in the morning.
I believe many people have had the same experience as me.
Short videos have indeed given us a lot: learn a dish in three minutes, catch up on a news story in one minute, and countless moments that make us laugh out loud.
But they have also quietly changed us: I find it harder and harder to finish a book, and even a two-hour movie feels too long.
Our patience seems to have been swiped away, one second at a time.
Some people say we should delete these apps; I have tried three times, and failed every time.
Later I switched to another method: not quitting completely, but setting a rule to watch only half an hour after dinner.
Even if I only manage half of it, it is still better than lying in bed scrolling until midnight.
Tools are neither good nor bad; the key question is: in the end, are we using them, or are they using us?
Nowadays, studying abroad is nothing new — every year hundreds of thousands of students go overseas to study.
The benefits of studying abroad are obvious: you can improve your foreign language, learn about different cultures, and train your ability to live independently.
Alone in a foreign country, you have to handle everything yourself, which makes many overseas students become more mature.
But studying abroad also has a side of loss.
First, studying abroad is very expensive and may use up money that parents have saved over many years.
Second, overseas students are far from their families and often feel especially homesick during the holidays.
In addition, after graduating, some people neither understand the situation back home nor can fully enter the foreign society, feeling that they stand between two cultures.
Therefore, whether or not to study abroad should be decided according to your own situation, not just by watching what others do.
In recent decades, more and more people from the countryside have left their hometowns to work and live in big cities.
This phenomenon is called "urbanization," and it is changing our society.
On the one hand, cities can offer more job opportunities and better educational conditions.
In the city, young people find it easier to realize their dreams, and incomes are much higher than in the countryside.
But on the other hand, urbanization has also brought quite a few problems, such as crowded traffic, expensive rent, and environmental pollution.
Meanwhile, only the elderly and children remain in the villages, and much of the land has no one to farm it.
Some people think we should keep developing big cities, while others advocate putting more resources into the countryside.
In my view, both cities and the countryside need to develop; the key is to let people freely choose their own way of life.
As a child, I thought happiness was simply scoring one hundred on a test, so Mom would buy me candy.
After growing up, happiness seemed to turn into a competition: a better job, a bigger house, a more expensive car.
But strangely, the more we obtain, the more anxious we become instead.
Last year I fell ill and stayed in the hospital for two weeks.
Lying in the hospital bed, I suddenly understood: being able to walk on my own, eat a proper meal, sleep a sound night's sleep — it turns out these are all happiness.
The day I left the hospital, the sunshine fell on my face and I almost cried.
Scientists say people quickly get used to the feeling of happiness; this is called "adaptation".
So happiness is not a result, but an ability: the ability to notice the small good things in life.
Rather than envying other people's lives, it is better to earnestly live your own today well.
What is happiness? Perhaps it is hiding in the cup of hot tea you drink today.
With high-rises standing like forests and streets streaming with traffic, big cities attract countless dream-chasing young people with their bustle and prosperity.
Behind the prosperity, however, 'big-city disease' is becoming increasingly prominent.
Traffic congestion devours people's time, and soaring housing prices leave young people gasping for breath.
Air pollution and noise disturbance further leave urban residents' quality of life greatly compromised.
Some choose to flee 'Beijing, Shanghai, and Guangzhou' and return to smaller cities with a gentler pace.
Others believe that the opportunities and resources of big cities remain irreplaceable.
In fact, the root of the problem lies not in city size itself, but in the imbalance of resource distribution.
Only by promoting coordinated regional development and making smaller cities equally good places to live and work can people truly have the freedom to choose.
Should a city's value, after all, be measured by its height, or by its residents' sense of happiness?
In traditional Chinese culture, collective interests are often placed above individual interests.
As the saying goes, 'the flame leaps high when everyone gathers firewood' — unity and cooperation can indeed create achievements no individual could ever reach.
However, overemphasizing the collective inevitably suppresses individuality, preventing personal creativity from being fully brought into play.
Conversely, Western societies uphold individualism, yet as a result face the predicament of fading human warmth and declining social cohesion.
From this we can see that the collective and the individual are by no means incompatible like fire and water, but rather complement each other.
A healthy society must both safeguard individual freedom and dignity, and cultivate citizens' sense of responsibility.
The key lies in striking the right balance: the collective should not engulf the individual, nor should the individual place itself above the collective.
So when collective and individual interests clash, how exactly should we choose between them?
Bankers understand the power of compound interest: let a small sum keep rolling over, and in a few decades it becomes a considerable fortune.
Habits, in fact, obey the very same law.
Twenty minutes of reading a day adds up to over a hundred hours a year — enough to chew through a dozen thick books.
Conversely, an hour of late-night scrolling every day, accumulated over months and years, overdraws your entire store of energy and health.
The frightening thing about a habit is that its effects are nearly invisible in the short run; good or bad, they only reveal themselves years later.

The British cycling team was once an obscure squad; its new coach pursued no grand reforms, demanding only a one-percent improvement in every detail — and within a few years the team was, astonishingly, sweeping Olympic medals.
Clearly, what determines the course of a life is seldom the grand, dramatic decisions, but the unremarkable little choices made every day.
A habit is a compound-interest account that time opens in our name: whatever you deposit today, you will harvest tomorrow.
In the Tao Te Ching, Laozi wrote: "He who knows contentment is rich."
Read today, more than two thousand years later, these words remain deeply thought-provoking.
Our material conditions far surpass those of the past, yet our sense of happiness has not necessarily grown with them.
Psychologists call this "hedonic adaptation": the thrill of a new car evaporates within three months, and people promptly fix their eyes on the next target.
I know an old carpenter in the countryside who lives in an old house and works with old tools, yet is always beaming; he says: "Enough is enough — craving more only wears you out."
Contentment is not a lack of ambition; it is knowing exactly what is worth chasing and what is merely the burden of vanity.
Desire is like seawater — the more you drink, the thirstier you become; gratitude is like a clear spring — a gentle stream that never runs dry.
Happiness was never on some faraway wish list; it lies in the everyday things we see the moment we lower our heads.
In today's fast-paced society, the term "craftsman spirit" seems somewhat out of step with the times.
And yet it is precisely those craftsmen — patient with solitude, forever striving for perfection — who have created countless breathtaking works.
In Japan there is a sushi master named Jiro Ono; after more than seventy years of making sushi, he is still pondering how to get the temperature of the rice exactly right.
When someone asked him the secret of his success, he said only this: "Do simple things over and over, and do the things you repeat with your whole heart."
The craftsman spirit is by no means stubborn conservatism; it is a pursuit of quality that borders on the ruthless.
At the same time, it is an attitude toward life: refusing to be swayed by restless trends, and focusing on each step of the work at hand.
In an age when everyone chases instant results, slowing down has, paradoxically, become a rare ability.
Craft skills may age, but the craftsman's heart never dies.
For the past several decades, globalization was regarded as an irreversible tide of history.
Goods, capital, and talent flowed freely across borders, as if the world had become a 'global village.'
In recent years, however, trade protectionism has reared its head, and quite a few countries have built tariff barriers one after another.
The shock of the pandemic further made countries realize that over-reliance on global supply chains harbors enormous risks.
Some people therefore assert that globalization has reached its end.
In my view, this claim is rather an exaggeration.
What is receding is only the old model; in the face of shared challenges such as climate change, the necessity of cooperation is only increasing.
Globalization will not die out; rather, it is seeking a new balance amid twists and turns.
Facing the receding tide, do we choose to build walls, or to build bridges?
In the 1960s, psychologists at Stanford University conducted the famous "marshmallow experiment."
The researchers gave each child a marshmallow and told them: if you can hold out for fifteen minutes without eating it, you will get a second one.
Some children stuffed the candy into their mouths at once; others shut their eyes or sang to distract themselves, and by sheer willpower endured the long wait.
Decades of follow-up showed that the children who could delay gratification generally went on to do better in both school and career.
Delaying gratification is not repressing desire; it is knowing clearly that some rewards are worth trading patience for.
Regrettably, modern life tempts us at every turn to "enjoy it now": takeout arrives in half an hour, short videos switch every three seconds, and even knowledge is sold as "mastered in ten minutes."
The more easily a pleasure is gained, the more quickly it vanishes; the longer a fruit must be waited for, the sweeter it tastes.
Life is like brewing wine: no fierce flame can force out the mellow aroma — only time can bring the flavor to perfection.
The moment 'the Mean' is mentioned, many people think of mediocrity, slickness, even spineless fence-sitting.
This is truly a misunderstanding that has lasted two thousand years.
The Mean that Confucius spoke of centers on 'excess is as bad as deficiency': overdoing a thing and underdoing it are equally harmful.
The Mean is by no means the absence of a stance; it is finding, between two extremes, the fulcrum that is exactly right.
It is like simmering a pot of soup: too fierce a flame boils it dry, too low a flame leaves it raw — what a good chef cares about is the control of heat.
In modern life this sense of proportion is everywhere: work hard, but not to the point of overdrawing your health; be sincere with people, but you need not be brutally blunt in every sentence.
In the internet age, black-or-white debates prevail and people rush to pick a side; the middle ground, paradoxically, has become the loneliest place to stand.
Yet truth seldom lives in the loudspeakers at either end; it lives in the quiet middle.
The Mean is not lacking the courage to go to extremes; it is having the power to go to extremes and choosing restraint — and that is what makes it so refined.
On the subway, nearly everyone's head is bowed over a phone, fingertips flying across the screen.
We take in more information every day than in any previous era, yet the knowledge that actually settles in us is pitifully scarce.
The greatest harm of fragmented reading lies in how it robs us of the capacity for deep thought.
A long essay gets squeezed into a three-line summary, a classic gets boiled down to a five-minute video — and just like that, depth of thought is sacrificed.
Neuroscience research shows that a brain long accustomed to skipping and skimming gradually loses the patience to concentrate for extended stretches.

This is not to say fragmented reading is worthless — for picking up information it is admittedly convenient — but letting it replace systematic reading is like living on snacks instead of proper meals.
Reading a thick book is like holding a long conversation: you must slow your pace, weighing and debating with the author again and again.
The more violently the tide of information surges, the more we need a quiet island where we can simply read.
The phrase 'great talent matures late' comes from the Tao Te Ching, meaning that true greatness often needs long years to ripen.
In an era that worships early success, these four characters feel like a dose of good medicine.
The painter Qi Baishi did not settle in Beijing until he was fifty-seven, was still reinventing his style at nearly sixty, and ultimately became the master of his generation.
He was by no means exceptionally gifted; rather, he honed his brushwork day after day for decades, slowly simmering loneliness into mastery.
Look at today, by contrast: many young people, if they have not 'succeeded' by thirty, conclude that they will never amount to anything.
In truth, life is not a sprint but a long marathon, and a slow starter does not necessarily lose at the finish line.
Society's clock is ticking, admittedly, but each of us lives in a time zone of our own.
Some flowers bloom in spring, while others wait until late autumn before bursting into full flower.
Rather than agonize over your age, quietly keep cultivating your craft; a great vessel is finished late precisely because it is so great.
In the era of information explosion, everyone is a receiver of information and, at the same time, a disseminator.
Social media has brought unprecedented convenience, but it has also given rumors wings.
A sensational piece of fake news often spreads faster and wider than a rigorous refutation.
Algorithms push content based on users' preferences, causing people to sink unknowingly into an 'information cocoon.'
Over time, people can only take in voices that match their own position, and social consensus becomes ever harder to form.
Media literacy has therefore become an essential basic ability for modern citizens.
It requires us to verify sources and distinguish truth from falsehood, rather than repost blindly.
Facing the flood of information on our screens, have you ever wondered: are we using the media, or is the media shaping us?
The ancients said: "Dripping water pierces stone."
A single drop of water is insignificant, yet day after day it can pierce through solid stone.
Learning a language is the same: it relies not on momentary enthusiasm, but on long-term accumulation.
Improving a little every day seems tiny, yet it will eventually converge into astonishing change.
So please believe in the power of persistence.
Two thousand years ago, an old man lived at the frontier; people called him Saiweng, and one day his horse ran off for no reason at all.
His neighbors came one after another to console him, but he said mildly: 'This is not necessarily a bad thing.'
Later, the horse returned leading a fine steed; his son broke his leg riding it; and when war broke out, the lame son was spared from conscription and kept his life.
The textbooks tell us the moral of the story is that 'fortune and misfortune depend on each other.'
But in my view, Saiweng's real gift was not foreseeing the future — it was his refusal to jump to conclusions.
Today we scroll past a piece of news and within seconds rush to like it or rage at it, slapping a label of 'good' or 'bad' on everything.
Yet life is a long film, and we keep trying to judge the entire plot from a single screenshot.
Saiweng's two little words — 'not necessarily' — are a rare kind of mental white space, leaving time itself room to explain.
The next time fate knocks at your door, take a page from the old man: don't applaud just yet — and don't sigh just yet either.
'The great way is utterly simple' comes from Daoist thought: the most fundamental truths of the universe can usually be stated in the plainest words.
Steve Jobs believed this deeply: he pared the phone down to a single button and hid all the complexity out of sight.
Simple, he said, is harder than complex, because you must polish your thinking until it is clear enough.
Our own lives, by contrast, are endless exercises in addition: wardrobes stuffed with clothes, calendars packed with meetings, phones loaded with apps.
We mistakenly assume that the more we own, the closer we are to happiness — and end up so crushed by material craving that we can hardly breathe.
In truth, cutting the complicated down to the simple is a higher-order skill: knowing what you do not want is harder than knowing what you want.
A mountain never explains its height, and the sea never shows off its depth.
True abundance often appears wearing the plainest face.
Only those who have learned to subtract can be said to have truly understood that the great way is simple.
The rapid development of science and technology is changing human life at an unprecedented speed.
Artificial intelligence can diagnose diseases, and gene editing promises to cure stubborn hereditary illnesses — the prospects are inspiring.
However, technology is a double-edged sword, and the consequences of abusing it are equally unthinkable.
Algorithms may intensify bias, facial recognition may infringe on privacy, and once data is leaked it can never be recalled, like spilt water.
What is even more worrying is that the pace of technological iteration far exceeds the pace at which law and morality are updated.
Some advocate that technology is neutral, believing the problem lies not in the tool but in the person who uses it.
But in my view, designers ought to build ethical considerations into technology from the very beginning.
The ultimate purpose of technology is to benefit humanity, not to turn people into slaves of their tools.
When technology can do everything, we need all the more to keep asking: what should not be done?
Legend has it that Yugong of the North Mountain was nearly ninety, yet two great mountains, Taihang and Wangwu, lay across his doorway, forcing a long detour every time he went out.
So he led his sons and grandsons in chiseling rock and digging earth, resolved to move the two mountains away.
Zhisou of Hequ ridiculed him for overreaching, but Yugong replied: 'My descendants will go on without end, whereas the mountains will grow no taller — why worry that they cannot be leveled?'
The Emperor of Heaven, moved by his sincerity, ordered mighty gods to carry the mountains away on their backs.
As a child, I was taught that the story is about perseverance; as an adult, I read another layer of meaning in it.
Modern people love to run a cost-benefit analysis before doing anything: how many years to learn a language? How long before workouts show results?
The moment the payoff comes too slowly, we give up halfway and turn to hunt for shortcuts.
What makes Yugong admirable is precisely that he never asked 'Will I live to see the result?' — only 'Is this worth beginning?'
Perhaps the mountain can never be fully moved — but a person no longer held hostage by immediate gains and losses has already climbed the highest mountain of all, the one within.

us: 在我看来
'In my view / as I see it' — introduces the speaker's personal opinion, usually at the start of a concluding sentence.
依我看
'in my view' — introduces the writer's own opinion, often before a counter-argument.
家 + 有 + number + 口 + 人
口 is the measure word for counting family members.
家 + 有 + number + 口 + 人
口 is the measure word for counting family members.
Place + 里/前面 + 有 — there is / there are
To say something exists at a place, put the place word first, then 有: 学校里有很多同学。学校前面有一个商店。
家 + 有 + number + 口 + 人
口 is the measure word for counting family members.
口 — the measure word for family members
When counting people in a family, Chinese uses 口 instead of 个: 四口人. Asking about it: 你家有几口人?
家 + 有 + number + 口 + 人
口 is the measure word for counting family members.
有人…,有人… — some people…, others…
Repeat 有人 to describe a lively scene: 有人跑步,有人唱歌 (some are jogging, some are singing).
A 和 B + verb
和 links two people doing something together: 'A does something with B'.
在 + place + verb
The action's location goes before the verb with 在: 我的猫在房间里睡觉 (my cat sleeps in the room), not at the end like English.
把 + object + 留在 + place
The 把 structure highlights where something ends up being left or kept: 工作把我留在了这里；把心留在了远方。
verb + 了
了 right after a verb marks the action as completed.
…了 — a new situation
了 at the end of the sentence signals a change of state: 豆豆两岁了 = Doudou has turned two (it wasn't two before).
对……来说
"For / as far as X is concerned" gives whose point of view the statement is true from: 对很多年轻人来说，咖啡不只是一种饮料。
很 + adjective
Chinese adjectives usually take 很 before them instead of using 是: 米米很小 = Mimi is small.

还是 vs 或者 — two kinds of 'or'
In QUESTIONS use 还是: 热的还是冰的? In statements use 或者: 我喝茶或者咖啡都可以.
不只是……更是……
'not only... but even more...' — the second part is the deeper or more important point.
与此同时
'At the same time; meanwhile' — a formal connector that adds a second, parallel statement to the previous sentence.
并非……,而是……
'is by no means..., but rather...' — a formal written pattern that rejects one description and asserts another.
并非…，而是…
A formal, emphatic way to say 'is by no means A, but rather B'; 并非 is stronger and more literary than 不是. 例：匠人精神并非守旧，而是对品质的追求。
无论……都……
'No matter... (still)...' — the result holds true in every case.
无论…，都…
'No matter / regardless of A, B holds.' 无论 is followed by a question word or paired alternatives like 好坏、多少. 例：无论好坏，都要多年以后才会显现。
被视为
'to be regarded as' — a formal passive structure very common in written Chinese.
往往
'Often / tend to' — states a general tendency based on experience, not a single event: 从旧工作到新工作的路往往要走很多年。
疑问词 + 都/也 (question word for 'any/every')
A question word + 都/也 + (不/没) means 'everyone/anyone', 'everything/anything': 谁都不认识 (not know anyone), 什么都想吃 (want to eat everything).
意味着
'To mean / to imply' — states the consequence of a fact: 这意味着能源、交通、工业都要发生巨大的变化。
虽然…但是…
"Although ... but ..." — both halves are used together in Chinese: 虽然我唱得不好，但是我很快乐。
不但 A,还 B — not only… but also…
The second half must add MORE on top of the first: 不但会用筷子,还会做中国菜.
哪怕…也…
'Even if… still…' — concedes an extreme or minimal case: 哪怕只是关掉灯，也算参与了。
哪怕…也… (even if…, still…)
哪怕 concedes an extreme or imperfect situation, and 也 states that the conclusion still holds: 哪怕只能做到一半，也比刷到半夜好。
……的时候 — when / while …
Put 的时候 after an action to say "while doing it": 看电影的时候 (while watching the movie).
与此同时
'At the same time; meanwhile' — a formal connector that adds a second, parallel statement to the previous sentence.
先…然后… (first…, then…)
先 + action 1, 然后 + action 2 puts actions in order: 先刷牙洗脸，然后吃早饭。
'After ..., (then) ...' — 以后 follows a time or action; 就 signals that the next thing happens right away.
一点儿 — a little
一点儿 goes after the verb to soften the amount: 我会说一点儿汉语 = I can speak a little Chinese.
adj + 一点儿 (a little more...)
一点儿 after an adjective softens it: a bit cheaper, a bit bigger.
反而
'On the contrary / instead' — the result is the opposite of what you'd expect: 少买东西以后，我反而觉得轻松了。
反而 (on the contrary)
反而 marks a result opposite to what one would expect: 得到的越多，我们反而越焦虑。
所谓的……
'The so-called...' — introduces a term that you are about to define or explain.
'If you want to..., then...' — states a goal and the way to achieve it.
……指的是……
'... refers to ...' — used to define a term or expression.
关键在于……
'The key lies in...' — points out the decisive factor for solving a problem.
关键在于……
'The key lies in...' — points out the decisive factor for solving a problem.
恰恰在于
'lies precisely in' — pinpoints the essence of something, often after words like 可贵、问题、区别. 愚公的可贵，恰恰在于他不问结果。
与其…不如… (rather than A, better B)
Compares two options and prefers the second: 与其在大城市给别人打工，不如回家做自己的事业。
与其…不如…
'Rather than A, better B' — compares two options and prefers the second: 与其在大城市继续竞争，不如回老家。
与其…不如… (rather than A, better B)

与其…不如…
'Rather than A, better B' — compares two options and prefers the second: 与其在大城市继续竞争，不如回老家。
一旦…就… (once…, then…)
一旦 marks a condition that, once it happens, brings an irreversible result: 有些东西一旦失去，就再也找不回来了。
一旦……便……
'once... then...' — a formal conditional often signaling serious or irreversible consequences.
靠 (to rely on)
靠 + noun expresses the means by which something is achieved: 他靠一双手把两个孩子养大了。
Place + 里/前面 + 有 — there is / there are
To say something exists at a place, put the place word first, then 有: 学校里有很多同学。学校前面有一个商店。
一方面……另一方面……
'On the one hand... on the other hand' — presents two contrasting sides of an issue, very common in argumentative writing.
对……满意
'Satisfied with ...' — 对 introduces the target of the feeling.
凡是…都… (every… without exception)
凡是 introduces a whole category, and 都 confirms the rule applies to all of it: 凡是好吃的面，后面都是几十年的耐心。
万一
'What if / in case' — introduces an unlikely but worrying possibility: 万一以后用得着呢？
把 + O + V + 了一遍
The 把 structure moves the object before the verb; 一遍 means doing the action once from start to finish.
愈发
'increasingly; all the more' — a written synonym of 越来越.
难道……吗？
A rhetorical question that expresses disbelief: 难道你觉得我射箭不好吗？
到底 (after all; in the end)
In a question, 到底 presses for the real answer: 到底是我们在用它，还是它在用我们？
既不……又不……
'Neither... nor...' — links two negative situations that are true at the same time.
'both... and...' — links two parallel requirements or qualities in one sentence.
根据……来决定
'Decide according to...' — base a decision on a certain standard or situation.
一方面……另一方面……
'On the one hand... on the other hand' — presents two contrasting sides of an issue, very common in argumentative writing.
一方面……另一方面……
'On the one hand... on the other hand' — presents two contrasting sides of an issue, very common in argumentative writing.
在我看来
'In my view / as I see it' — introduces the speaker's personal opinion, usually at the start of a concluding sentence.
…，所以… — result
所以 introduces the result of a reason: 公司离家很远，所以我坐公共汽车. Often paired with 因为.
'After ..., (then) ...' — 以后 follows a time or action; 就 signals that the next thing happens right away.
首先……其次……另外……最后……
A set of connectors for listing points in order: 首先 (first), 其次 (second), 另外 (in addition), 最后 (finally). The text uses them to organize the interview tips.
看起来……其实……
'it seems... but actually...' — contrasts appearance with reality.
使 + sb + verb
'to cause/make somebody do something' — a formal causative common in written argumentation.
与其…不如… (rather than A, better B)

与其…不如…
'Rather than A, better B' — compares two options and prefers the second: 与其在大城市继续竞争，不如回老家。
不在……,而在……
'lies not in..., but in...' — rejects a false cause and points to the true one.
不仅……而且……
"Not only A, but also B" — B goes further than A: 高铁改变的不仅是速度，而且是人们的生活。
不再……而是……
'no longer A, but instead B' — an old way replaced by a new one.
唯有……才……
'only by... can...' — a formal conditional stating the sole path to a result.
并非……,而是……

关键在于……
'The key lies in...' — points out the decisive factor for solving a problem.
无异于
'To be no different from; tantamount to' — a formal comparison used to condemn or dramatize: doing A is effectively doing B. 例：以碎片化阅读取代读书，无异于用零食代替正餐。
一旦……便……
'once... then...' — a formal conditional often signaling serious or irreversible consequences.
一旦……，便/就……
'Once/the moment A happens, then B' — 便 is the literary equivalent of 就. 一旦回报来得太慢，我们便半途而废。
反之
'Conversely; on the other hand' — a formal connector that introduces the opposite case of what was just said. 例：每天读书使人进步；反之，每天熬夜刷手机透支健康。

Literary 者
Classical 者 turns a verb or phrase into 'one who…', common in proverbs and formal writing. 例：知足者富 = 知足的人富有。
未必
'Not necessarily' — a softer, more literary way to negate certainty than 不一定. 例：物质条件好了，幸福感却未必增长。
与此同时
'At the same time; meanwhile' — a formal connector that adds a second, parallel statement to the previous sentence.
被视为
'to be regarded as' — a formal passive structure very common in written Chinese.
依我看
'in my view' — introduces the writer's own opinion, often before a counter-argument.
未免
'rather; a bit too' — politely signals that a statement or judgment goes too far.
A…，B 则…
Formal 则 marks a contrast between two subjects: 'A does X, while B does Y'. 例：有的孩子马上吃了糖，有的则熬过了等待。
Literary 则 contrasts two parallel clauses ('whereas; then'): 火太猛会烧干，火太小则煮不熟。
越是…，越是…
An emphatic version of 越…越… : 'the more (it is) A, the more (it is) B', often used for general truths. 例：越是容易得到的快乐，越是转瞬即逝。

固然…，但…
